text.title: User Control and Freedom Core Rules
text.summary: Testable rules grounding User Control and Freedom. Each pairs a binary check with a sourced rationale; HARD rules — user-supplied input cannot override.

rule.statement: Every destructive action exposes either an immediate undo or a clearly labelled confirmation with a recoverable trash for at least 30 days.
rule.rationale: Without undo, users avoid the feature or pay a heavy mistake tax.

rule.statement: Modals, multi-step flows, and full-screen takeovers carry an explicit close affordance — Esc, X icon, and back swipe — that does not lose entered data.
rule.rationale: Trapped states are a frequent rage-click trigger.

rule.statement: A single confirmation per destructive action is enough; cascading confirm dialogs train users to click through them.
rule.rationale: Confirmation fatigue defeats the safety the dialog was supposed to provide.

rule.statement: Where workflows support undo, they also support redo and a visible history so users can navigate the stack confidently.
rule.rationale: Undo without redo punishes accidental over-undo.

rule.statement: Pressing Esc, swiping back, or tapping the system back button always honours user intent — never silently committing pending changes.
rule.rationale: Silent commits on cancel violate user expectation.

rule.statement: If preconditions in AGENTS.md "Applies If" do not all hold, OR any "Skip If" disqualifier is true, do not run this methodology — route to the named alternative.
rule.rationale: Methodologies are pre-condition gated; running them out-of-scope produces noise and burns the agent context budget.

rule.statement: If preconditions hold, execute the procedure in `content/04-procedure.xml` (or the rule set above when complexity is light), emit the artefact specified by `content/02-output-contract.xml`, and validate with `scripts/validate-user-control-freedom.py` before returning.
rule.rationale: Tying the entry rule to the validator script closes the loop between policy and machine check.
